question: What are the six Annexes of MARPOL and which are mandatory?
key_points: Annex I: Oil — mandatory (entered into force 1983)
Annex II: Noxious Liquid Substances in Bulk — mandatory (entered into force 1987)
Annex III: Harmful Substances in Packaged Form — mandatory (entered into force 1992)
Annex IV: Sewage — mandatory (entered into force 2003)
Annex V: Garbage — mandatory (entered into force 1988)
Annex VI: Air Pollution — mandatory (entered into force 2005)
All six annexes are now in force — Annexes I and II are compulsory for signatories, III-VI are optional
follow_ups: What is an Emission Control Area under Annex VI?
examiner_notes: Candidates should know each annex and its general scope. Annexes I, II, IV, V, and VI designate Special Areas with stricter requirements. The Baltic, North Sea, and Mediterranean are Special Areas under multiple annexes.

question: What are the requirements for oil discharge from machinery spaces under MARPOL Annex I?
key_points: Regulation 15 — discharge from machinery spaces of ships >= 400 GT
Oil content must not exceed 15 ppm (parts per million)
Discharge through approved oil filtering equipment with 15 ppm alarm and automatic stopping device
Ship must be proceeding en route (not stationary)
Oil Record Book Part I must record all operations
No discharge of oil into Special Areas — more restrictive requirements apply
follow_ups: What must be recorded in the Oil Record Book?
What are the Special Area requirements under Annex I?
examiner_notes: Candidates must distinguish between machinery space discharges (Regulation 15) and cargo area discharges from tankers (Regulation 34). The 15 ppm limit, approved equipment, and Oil Record Book entries are frequently examined. Port State Control officers specifically check ORB entries.

question: Describe the garbage management requirements under MARPOL Annex V.
key_points: General prohibition on discharge of all garbage into the sea (Regulation 3)
Limited exceptions: food waste (comminuted, > 3nm from land, > 12nm in Special Areas), cargo residues not harmful to the marine environment
All ships >= 100 GT or carrying 15+ persons must carry a Garbage Management Plan
All ships >= 400 GT must maintain a Garbage Record Book
Garbage categories must be recorded: plastics, food waste, domestic waste, cooking oil, operational waste, cargo residues, animal carcasses, fishing gear, e-waste
Discharge of all plastics is prohibited everywhere
follow_ups: What are the additional restrictions in Special Areas?
What are the garbage placards requirements?
examiner_notes: The 2013 amendments significantly tightened Annex V — the default is now no discharge permitted, with limited exceptions. Candidates must know that plastics are never permitted to be discharged. Garbage Record Book retention period is 2 years from last entry.

question: What are the current sulphur emission limits under MARPOL Annex VI?
key_points: Global sulphur cap reduced to 0.50% m/m from 1 January 2020 (previously 3.50%)
Emission Control Areas (ECAs): 0.10% m/m sulphur limit
Current ECAs: Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American ECA, US Caribbean ECA
Compliance options: low-sulphur fuel oil, approved exhaust gas cleaning systems (scrubbers), alternative fuels (LNG)
Fuel oil non-availability report (FONAR) procedure if compliant fuel unavailable
Ships >= 5000 GT must have a Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP)
follow_ups: What is the EEDI and which ships must comply?
What are the implications of using open-loop scrubbers in port?
examiner_notes: The 2020 sulphur cap is a landmark regulation. Candidates should know practical compliance options and the carriage ban (Regulation 14.1.3) which prohibits carriage of non-compliant fuel unless using scrubbers. EEXI and CII measures from 2023 amendments should also be discussed.

question: Explain the Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan (SOPEP) requirements.
key_points: Required under MARPOL Annex I Regulation 37 for all ships >= 400 GT
Must be approved by the flag state administration
Contains: reporting procedures, contact details for authorities, actions to control discharge
Must list coastal state contacts and port authority contacts for intended route
Crew must be trained in SOPEP procedures — drills required
Must be coordinated with the Shipboard Marine Pollution Emergency Plan (SMPEP) for Annex II ships
follow_ups: Who must be notified in the event of an oil spill?
What immediate actions should be taken when an oil spill is discovered?
examiner_notes: SOPEP is one of the most frequently inspected documents during Port State Control. Candidates should know reporting obligations under MARPOL Protocol I and be able to describe the contents and practical application of the plan.
